sim-t1003-lsass-mimikatz-canary.adversary_activity: Reading `lsass.exe` memory extracts plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, and Kerberos tickets (Mimikatz `sekurlsa::logonpasswords`, `procdump -ma lsass`, `rundll32 comsvcs.dll MiniDump`, task-manager dump, `nanodump`). The fixture posts process/command-line-shaped telemetry but never touches LSASS memory.
sim-t1003-lsass-mimikatz-canary.detection_logic: Alert on any non-allowlisted process opening `lsass.exe` with read/VM access rights, on `comsvcs.dll`+`MiniDump` via rundll32, on `procdump ... lsass`, and on `sekurlsa`/`lsadump`/`mimikatz` strings. `GrantedAccess` to LSASS combined with an unusual `SourceImage` is the core signal.
sim-t1003-lsass-mimikatz-canary.discriminators_tuning: AV/EDR, `MsMpEng.exe`, and some monitoring legitimately access LSASS — maintain a tight allowlist of those `SourceImage`s; anything outside it accessing LSASS memory is high-severity.
sim-t1003-lsass-mimikatz-canary.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 10 (ProcessAccess targeting `lsass.exe` with `GrantedAccess` masks like `0x1010`/`0x1410`/`0x143A`), EID 1 (dumping-tool command lines), Defender ASR "block credential stealing from LSASS", EDR credential-theft telemetry, EID 11 (an `.dmp` written).
sim-t1003-lsass-mimikatz-canary.title: LSASS and Mimikatz usage canary

sim-t1003-shadow-file-access.adversary_activity: Reading `/etc/shadow` (and `/etc/gshadow`) yields hashed credentials for offline cracking (`cat`/`cp`/`awk` on shadow, copying it out). The fixture posts process/file telemetry but never reads the real file.
sim-t1003-shadow-file-access.detection_logic: Add an auditd watch on `/etc/shadow` and `/etc/gshadow` and alert on read/copy by any non-system process or any interactive user, on `cat`/`cp`/`tar`/`scp` referencing shadow, and on shadow access by a web/service account. Read of shadow by an unexpected UID is high-fidelity.
sim-t1003-shadow-file-access.discriminators_tuning: `passwd`, `vipw`, PAM, and backup jobs touch shadow legitimately — allowlist those binaries/paths and alert on ad-hoc reads.
sim-t1003-shadow-file-access.production_log_sources: Linux `auditd` (file-watch on `/etc/shadow`: `open`/`openat`/`read` syscalls with the path), `auditd` `execve` for the reading command, EDR file-access telemetry, `/var/log/audit/audit.log`.
sim-t1003-shadow-file-access.title: Linux /etc/shadow access canary

sim-t1016-network-config-discovery.adversary_activity: Enumerating local network configuration to orient — `ipconfig /all`, `netsh interface show`, `route print`, `arp -a`, `Get-NetIPConfiguration`. The fixture records process telemetry without querying networking.
sim-t1016-network-config-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on `ipconfig`/`netsh`/`route`/`arp` executed by service/web accounts, spawned by Office/script hosts, or as part of a discovery burst alongside `whoami`/`systeminfo`.
sim-t1016-network-config-discovery.discriminators_tuning: Helpdesk, logon scripts, and troubleshooting run these — single executions by interactive admins are noise; context and clustering are the signal.
sim-t1016-network-config-discovery.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`Image`, `CommandLine`, `User`, `ParentImage`), Security `4688`; `auditd execve` on Linux for `ip`/`ifconfig`/`route`.
sim-t1016-network-config-discovery.title: System network configuration discovery

sim-t1018-remote-system-discovery.adversary_activity: Mapping other hosts/domain controllers for lateral movement — `net view`, `nltest /dclist`, `dsquery computer`, ping sweeps, `arp`. The fixture records process telemetry without querying the network.
sim-t1018-remote-system-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on `net view /domain`, `nltest /dclist`/`/domain_trusts`, `dsquery` enumeration, and scripted ping sweeps — particularly by non-admin/service contexts or in a recon burst.
sim-t1018-remote-system-discovery.discriminators_tuning: Admin and inventory tooling enumerate hosts — weight on unusual user context and co-occurrence with other discovery/lateral activity.
sim-t1018-remote-system-discovery.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`net.exe view`, `nltest.exe`, `dsquery.exe`), Security `4688`; on DCs, `4661`/`4662` directory-object access.
sim-t1018-remote-system-discovery.title: Remote system discovery

sim-t1021-001-atomic-rdp-network-logon.adversary_activity: An actor moves laterally via RDP using valid/stolen credentials, getting an interactive desktop on a remote host. The fixture emits a Security `4624 SuccessfulLogon`.
sim-t1021-001-atomic-rdp-network-logon.detection_logic: Alert on `4624` Type 10 from an unusual source workstation/IP, to a host the account never RDPs to, off-hours, or chained host-to-host (RDP pivoting). New `4778` sessions from internal hosts that don't normally originate RDP are a strong lateral signal.
sim-t1021-001-atomic-rdp-network-logon.discriminators_tuning: Admins and jump-host workflows use RDP heavily — baseline normal source→destination RDP pairs per account and alert on new pairs and internal lateral chains.
sim-t1021-001-atomic-rdp-network-logon.production_log_sources: Security `4624` LogonType **10** (RemoteInteractive) and **7** (reconnect), `4778`/`4779` (session connect/disconnect), TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager/Operational `1149`, TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager `21`/`25`, network firewall logs for 3389.
sim-t1021-001-atomic-rdp-network-logon.title: Atomic event: Remote Desktop Protocol logon

sim-t1021-002-atomic-smb-admin-share-acc.adversary_activity: Lateral movement and remote execution use the hidden admin shares (`C$`, `ADMIN$`, `IPC$`) to copy tools and run payloads (PsExec, `copy \\host\C$`). The fixture emits a Security `5140 NetworkShareAccess`.
sim-t1021-002-atomic-smb-admin-share-acc.detection_logic: Alert on access to `\\*\ADMIN$`/`\\*\C$`/`\\*\IPC$` from workstations (peer-to-peer admin-share access is abnormal), on a single source touching admin shares on many hosts, and on `5145` showing executable/tool writes to admin shares. Pairs with `7045`/`4624` Type 3 for PsExec-style movement.
sim-t1021-002-atomic-smb-admin-share-acc.discriminators_tuning: SCCM, backup, and admin tooling legitimately use admin shares from management servers — allowlist those servers and alert on workstation-originated and fan-out access. --- # Collection
sim-t1021-002-atomic-smb-admin-share-acc.production_log_sources: Security `5140` (share access) and `5145` (detailed file-share access with filename), `4624` Type 3 (the network logon enabling it), Sysmon EID 3, file-server audit logs.
sim-t1021-002-atomic-smb-admin-share-acc.title: Atomic event: SMB admin share access

sim-t1027-atomic-encoded-powershell-scri.adversary_activity: Payloads are obfuscated to evade signatures and analysts — base64/compressed/encoded script blocks, string concatenation, char-code arrays, and reflection. The fixture emits a PowerShell `4104 ScriptBlockLogging` event.
sim-t1027-atomic-encoded-powershell-scri.detection_logic: Alert on `4104` blocks with high entropy, large base64 strings, `FromBase64String`, `[char[]]`/`-join` reconstruction, `IEX`/`Invoke-Expression`, gzip/deflate stream creation, or backtick/case obfuscation. AMSI-deobfuscated script blocks reveal the true content for matching.
sim-t1027-atomic-encoded-powershell-scri.discriminators_tuning: Some legitimate tooling encodes scripts — allowlist by hash/signer and weight on multiple obfuscation indicators co-occurring.
sim-t1027-atomic-encoded-powershell-scri.production_log_sources: PowerShell Script Block Logging `4104`, Module Logging `4103`, Sysmon EID 1 command line, AMSI telemetry.
sim-t1027-atomic-encoded-powershell-scri.title: Atomic event: Obfuscated PowerShell script block

sim-t1033-user-discovery.adversary_activity: Determining who is logged on / current privileges — `whoami`, `whoami /priv /groups`, `query user`, `$env:USERNAME`. The fixture records process telemetry without running commands.
sim-t1033-user-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on `whoami` (especially `/priv`/`/groups`/`/all`) run by a **service account, web-service process, or right after exploitation** — that combination is far more suspicious than an admin typing `whoami`. Office/web-server parents are a strong tell.
sim-t1033-user-discovery.discriminators_tuning: Interactive users and scripts run `whoami` constantly — the parent process and account context carry the signal.
sim-t1033-user-discovery.title: System owner and user discovery

sim-t1036-atomic-masqueraded-svchost-use.adversary_activity: Malware names itself after a trusted system process (`svchost.exe`, `lsass.exe`, `services.exe`) but runs from the wrong location or with the wrong parent to blend in. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1036-atomic-masqueraded-svchost-use.detection_logic: Alert when a system-process name runs from a non-system path (e.g., `svchost.exe` not in `System32`/`SysWOW64`), when `Image` filename ≠ `OriginalFileName` (metadata mismatch), when `svchost.exe`'s parent is not `services.exe`, or when a "system" binary is unsigned. These are reliable masquerading tells.
sim-t1036-atomic-masqueraded-svchost-use.discriminators_tuning: Very few benign processes mimic system names from user paths — path/parent/signature checks make this clean. Watch for portable apps reusing generic names.
sim-t1036-atomic-masqueraded-svchost-use.title: Atomic event: Masqueraded system process from user-writable path

sim-t1039-atomic-network-share-data-acce.adversary_activity: Bulk-reading files from network shares to collect data for exfiltration. The fixture emits a Security `4663 ObjectAccess`.
sim-t1039-atomic-network-share-data-acce.detection_logic: Alert on a single account/host reading an unusually high volume of files across share directories in a short window, on access to sensitive shares the account doesn't normally use, and on staging (many reads followed by a local copy/archive). Volume + breadth anomaly is the signal.
sim-t1039-atomic-network-share-data-acce.discriminators_tuning: Normal file-server use generates constant `4663`/`5145` — this requires UEBA-style volume baselining per user; raw object access is not alertable. Note `4663` needs SACLs configured to fire at all.
sim-t1039-atomic-network-share-data-acce.production_log_sources: Security `4663` (object access — requires SACLs on the share), `5145` (file-share detailed access), file-server/NAS audit logs, EDR file-read telemetry, DLP.
sim-t1039-atomic-network-share-data-acce.title: Atomic event: Data from network shared drive

sim-t1041-web-exfil-canary.adversary_activity: Data is exfiltrated over the existing C2 channel — typically large/encoded POST bodies to a collection endpoint. The simulation POSTs small benign JSON/text bodies and records body metadata without storing content.
sim-t1041-web-exfil-canary.detection_logic: Alert on anomalously large or multi-line POST bodies to export/collection endpoints, on outbound byte volumes that exceed the host's baseline, and on encoded/compressed bodies leaving to a single destination. Upload-volume anomaly to a rare destination is the signal.
sim-t1041-web-exfil-canary.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate uploads (backups, file-sharing) are large too — baseline per-host outbound volume and weight on destination rarity and beacon correlation.
sim-t1041-web-exfil-canary.production_log_sources: Web proxy logs (outbound bytes, POST sizes), web access logs, NDR/flow (upload volume), DLP.
sim-t1041-web-exfil-canary.title: Web exfiltration-shaped upload canary

sim-t1047-atomic-wmic-process-call-creat.adversary_activity: Windows Management Instrumentation executes commands locally or remotely (`wmic process call create`, `Invoke-WmiMethod Win32_Process`), used for execution and lateral movement. Remotely-invoked WMI spawns children under `WmiPrvSE.exe`. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1047-atomic-wmic-process-call-creat.detection_logic: Alert on `wmic.exe ... process call create`, on `WmiPrvSE.exe` spawning `cmd.exe`/`powershell.exe` (strong remote-WMI-execution signal), and on `Invoke-WmiMethod`/`Invoke-CimMethod` with process creation. Remote WMI execution typically pairs with a `4624` network logon from the source host.
sim-t1047-atomic-wmic-process-call-creat.discriminators_tuning: Management/monitoring tooling uses WMI — `WmiPrvSE.exe` spawning interactive shells is rare and high-value; baseline legitimate WMI consumers.
sim-t1047-atomic-wmic-process-call-creat.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`Image=wmic.exe` or `ParentImage=WmiPrvSE.exe`), Security `4688`, **WMI-Activity/Operational** log (`5857`–`5861`) for provider/consumer activity.
sim-t1047-atomic-wmic-process-call-creat.title: Atomic event: WMI process creation

sim-t1048-atomic-exfiltration-over-alter.adversary_activity: Data leaves over a protocol separate from C2 — DNS tunneling, FTP/SFTP, ICMP, or raw sockets to non-standard ports — to evade web-centric controls. The fixture emits a structured firewall `NETFLOW`/`OutboundTransfer` event.
sim-t1048-atomic-exfiltration-over-alter.detection_logic: Alert on high-volume outbound to non-standard ports/protocols, on DNS exfil signatures (long high-entropy subdomains, abnormal query volume to one domain, TXT/NULL record abuse), and on FTP/ICMP carrying unusual data volumes. Egress that bypasses the proxy is itself suspicious.
sim-t1048-atomic-exfiltration-over-alter.discriminators_tuning: Some apps use non-HTTP protocols legitimately — baseline normal egress ports/protocols per host and alert on deviations, especially DNS volume/entropy anomalies.
sim-t1048-atomic-exfiltration-over-alter.production_log_sources: Firewall/NetFlow logs (bytes out, ports, protocols), DNS logs (query length/volume/entropy for DNS tunneling), NDR, proxy bypass detection.
sim-t1048-atomic-exfiltration-over-alter.title: Atomic event: Exfiltration over alternative protocol

sim-t1049-atomic-netstat-network-connect.adversary_activity: Listing active connections/listeners to find lateral targets and understand the host's role — `netstat -ano`, `Get-NetTCPConnection`, `ss`/`netstat` on Linux. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1049-atomic-netstat-network-connect.detection_logic: Alert on `netstat`/`Get-NetTCPConnection` by service/web accounts or in a discovery burst. On its own it's low-signal; valuable as part of a recon cluster.
sim-t1049-atomic-netstat-network-connect.discriminators_tuning: Extremely common in legitimate troubleshooting — only meaningful with anomalous context.
sim-t1049-atomic-netstat-network-connect.title: Atomic event: System network connection discovery

sim-t1053-scheduled-task.adversary_activity: A scheduled task re-launches a payload on a trigger (logon, time, idle) for persistence or runs it as SYSTEM for privilege escalation (`schtasks /create`, `Register-ScheduledTask`). The fixture records task-creation command lines without creating a task.
sim-t1053-scheduled-task.detection_logic: Alert on `schtasks /create` or `Register-ScheduledTask` whose action is a script host/LOLBin/binary in a user-writable path, on tasks running as `SYSTEM` created by a non-admin context, and on hidden tasks. Inspect the task's `Command`/`Arguments` in `4698` XML.
sim-t1053-scheduled-task.discriminators_tuning: Installers and management agents create tasks constantly — the action target (what runs) and its path are the discriminators; benign tasks point at signed binaries in Program Files.
sim-t1053-scheduled-task.production_log_sources: Security `4698` (task created), `4702` (task updated), Sysmon EID 1 (`Image=schtasks.exe` / PowerShell), **Task Scheduler/Operational** log (`106` registered, `140` updated, `200`/`201` action run).
sim-t1053-scheduled-task.title: Scheduled task creation

sim-t1055-atomic-remote-thread-injection.adversary_activity: Process injection writes code into a victim process and runs it via a remote thread (`CreateRemoteThread`, APC, thread hijacking), hiding malicious execution inside a trusted process. The fixture emits a Sysmon `8 CreateRemoteThread`.
sim-t1055-atomic-remote-thread-injection.detection_logic: Alert on cross-process remote thread creation where source and target are unrelated, on injections targeting sensitive processes (`lsass.exe`, `explorer.exe`, browsers), and on `StartAddress` pointing to unbacked/private memory. `ProcessAccess` masks like `0x1F3FFF`/`0x1FFFFF` to sensitive targets are flags.
sim-t1055-atomic-remote-thread-injection.discriminators_tuning: AV/EDR, debuggers, and some accessibility tools inject legitimately — allowlist known injectors and weight on target sensitivity and memory backing.
sim-t1055-atomic-remote-thread-injection.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 8 (CreateRemoteThread: `SourceImage`, `TargetImage`, `StartAddress`), EID 10 (ProcessAccess with `GrantedAccess` granting `VM_WRITE`/`CREATE_THREAD`), EDR injection telemetry.
sim-t1055-atomic-remote-thread-injection.title: Atomic event: Remote thread creation into another process

sim-t1056-001-atomic-keylogger-driver-or.adversary_activity: A keylogger captures keystrokes via a global keyboard hook (`SetWindowsHookEx WH_KEYBOARD_LL`), `GetAsyncKeyState` polling, raw-input registration, or a filter driver. The fixture emits a structured EDR `INPUT_CAPTURE`/`KeyboardHookRegistered` event.
sim-t1056-001-atomic-keylogger-driver-or.detection_logic: Alert on global low-level keyboard hook registration by non-UI/background processes, on tight `GetAsyncKeyState` polling loops, and on raw-input device registration by unexpected binaries. Pair with a file being written (the keystroke log) for confirmation.
sim-t1056-001-atomic-keylogger-driver-or.discriminators_tuning: Accessibility tools, macro/hotkey utilities, IMEs, and some games register keyboard hooks legitimately — allowlist those and weight on background processes with no UI registering global hooks.
sim-t1056-001-atomic-keylogger-driver-or.production_log_sources: EDR API/behavioral telemetry (hook registration, raw-input, key-state polling) — **not in Sysmon/Windows event logs**; driver-load events for kernel keyloggers.
sim-t1056-001-atomic-keylogger-driver-or.title: Atomic event: Keylogging hook artifact

sim-t1057-process-discovery.adversary_activity: Listing running processes to find security tooling, targets for injection, or valuable apps — `tasklist`, `Get-Process`, `ps aux`, `wmic process`. The fixture records process telemetry without enumerating the host.
sim-t1057-process-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on `tasklist`/`Get-Process` piped to `findstr`/`Select-String` for AV/EDR names (overlaps T1518.001), and on process discovery in a recon burst or by service accounts.
sim-t1057-process-discovery.discriminators_tuning: Ubiquitous benign use — the AV-name filtering variant and burst context are the useful slices.
sim-t1057-process-discovery.title: Process discovery

sim-t1059-005-atomic-visual-basic-script.adversary_activity: Malicious `.vbs`/`.vbe` scripts run via `wscript.exe`/`cscript.exe`, commonly as the payload of a phishing attachment or as a persistence/launcher. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1059-005-atomic-visual-basic-script.detection_logic: Alert on `wscript.exe`/`cscript.exe` executing scripts from `\AppData\`, `\Temp\`, `\Downloads\`, or removable media, and on Office/archive/email-client parents launching them. Long obfuscated VBS arguments are a flag.
sim-t1059-005-atomic-visual-basic-script.discriminators_tuning: Some enterprises rely on legacy VBS logon scripts and vendor installers — allowlist by path/hash and prioritize user-writable source directories and document parents.
sim-t1059-005-atomic-visual-basic-script.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`Image=wscript.exe`/`cscript.exe`, `CommandLine`, `ParentImage`), Security `4688`, Windows Script Host telemetry.
sim-t1059-005-atomic-visual-basic-script.title: Atomic event: Visual Basic script execution

sim-t1059-006-atomic-python-execution.adversary_activity: Cross-platform actors and offensive frameworks use Python for payloads and tooling (`python -c "<one-liner>"`, scripts from temp dirs, embedded interpreters). The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1059-006-atomic-python-execution.detection_logic: Alert on `python`/`python3` with inline `-c` one-liners (especially containing `socket`, `base64`, `exec`, `os.system`, `subprocess`), execution from `\Temp\`/`/tmp/`, or a Python interpreter shipped inside a user-app directory. Reverse-shell one-liners are a high-fidelity pattern.
sim-t1059-006-atomic-python-execution.discriminators_tuning: Developer and data-science hosts run Python constantly — scope by host role; on servers and standard user endpoints the signal is far cleaner than on engineering laptops.
sim-t1059-006-atomic-python-execution.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (Windows) / `auditd` `execve` (Linux/macOS) capturing `python`/`python3` with arguments, EDR process telemetry.
sim-t1059-006-atomic-python-execution.title: Atomic event: Python script execution

sim-t1059-cmd-shell-execution.adversary_activity: `cmd.exe /c` chains discovery, download, and execution commands, often spawned by a document, web service, or script. The fixture records command-line-shaped telemetry without creating a shell.
sim-t1059-cmd-shell-execution.detection_logic: Alert on `cmd.exe /c` chains combining discovery/download/exec (`whoami & net user & ...`), on `cmd.exe` spawned by Office/web-service/script parents, and on obfuscated cmd (caret `^`, env-var substitution, excessive quoting). Sequenced discovery in one command line is a strong recon-after-access signal.
sim-t1059-cmd-shell-execution.discriminators_tuning: `cmd.exe` is ubiquitous in legitimate scripts and installers — the parent process and command content carry all the signal; raw `cmd.exe` launches are not alertable.
sim-t1059-cmd-shell-execution.title: Windows command shell execution

sim-t1059-powershell-encoded-command.adversary_activity: PowerShell is the dominant Windows attack interpreter. Adversaries hide intent with `-EncodedCommand` (base64), `-WindowStyle Hidden`, `-NoProfile`, `-ExecutionPolicy Bypass`, and download-cradles (`IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString(...)`). The fixture emits Sysmon-style process-create events only.
sim-t1059-powershell-encoded-command.detection_logic: Alert on `powershell.exe`/`pwsh.exe` with `-enc`/`-EncodedCommand`, `-w hidden`, `-nop`, `-ep bypass`, and on `4104` script blocks containing `FromBase64String`, `IEX`, `DownloadString`, `Invoke-Expression`, `[char]` concatenation, or gzip/deflate decompression. High base64 entropy in the command line is a strong flag.
sim-t1059-powershell-encoded-command.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate management tooling (SCCM, RMM, installers) uses encoded commands — allowlist known-good encoded blobs by hash/parent, and weight on hidden+nop+bypass combinations.
sim-t1059-powershell-encoded-command.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`CommandLine`), PowerShell Script Block Logging (`4104`), Module Logging (`4103`), engine events (`400`/`600`), Security `4688` with command line auditing.
sim-t1059-powershell-encoded-command.title: PowerShell encoded command execution

sim-t1059-web-command-canary.adversary_activity: Command injection passes OS-shell metacharacters through a vulnerable web parameter so the server executes attacker commands (`; id`, `| whoami`, `$(...)`, backticks). The simulation sends command-shaped parameters and records body length/hash without ever executing them.
sim-t1059-web-command-canary.detection_logic: Alert on shell metacharacters and command keywords in parameters/bodies (`;`, `|`, `&&`, `$(`, `` ` ``, `whoami`, `cat /etc/passwd`, `ping -c`). The decisive production signal is the **web server process becoming the parent of `cmd.exe`/`/bin/sh`/`bash`**.
sim-t1059-web-command-canary.discriminators_tuning: Free-text fields can contain these characters — bind to parameters that reach a shell/exec sink and prioritize the parent-child process signal on the server.
sim-t1059-web-command-canary.production_log_sources: Web access logs and request bodies, WAF logs, app logs, and — for confirmation — endpoint Sysmon EID 1 showing the web service process (`w3wp.exe`, `php-fpm`, `node`, `httpd`) spawning a shell.
sim-t1059-web-command-canary.title: Web command execution canary validation

sim-t1068-atomic-privilege-escalation-ex.adversary_activity: Exploiting a vulnerability in a privileged process or driver yields code execution at higher integrity — typically observed as a privileged/SYSTEM service unexpectedly spawning a shell after a crash- or exploit-like event. The fixture emits a structured EDR `PRIV_ESC` event.
sim-t1068-atomic-privilege-escalation-ex.detection_logic: Alert when a service/host process running as `SYSTEM`/high integrity spawns `cmd.exe`/`powershell.exe`/`/bin/sh` unexpectedly, especially right after a crash of the same parent, and on a child process whose integrity level is higher than the parent's user context implies.
sim-t1068-atomic-privilege-escalation-ex.discriminators_tuning: Some legitimate service workflows spawn shells — baseline per-service child processes; an unexpected SYSTEM shell from a network-facing service is the high-value slice.
sim-t1068-atomic-privilege-escalation-ex.production_log_sources: EDR exploit/behavioral telemetry, Sysmon EID 1 (`ParentImage` = a service running as SYSTEM, `Image` = shell, integrity-level rise), Application/`WER` crash events preceding the child, Defender exploit-guard events.
sim-t1068-atomic-privilege-escalation-ex.title: Atomic event: Privileged service spawned shell after exploit-like event

sim-t1070-001-atomic-windows-event-log-c.adversary_activity: Clearing event logs destroys evidence of intrusion (`wevtutil cl`, `Clear-EventLog`, `Remove-EventLog`, or direct API). The fixture emits a Security `1102 AuditLogCleared`.
sim-t1070-001-atomic-windows-event-log-c.detection_logic: Alert on **any** `1102` or `104` — log clearing is rare and high-fidelity, and the event survives because it is written as the log is cleared. Correlate the clearing account/host and look for what activity preceded it.
sim-t1070-001-atomic-windows-event-log-c.discriminators_tuning: Reimaging and some maintenance clear logs — confirm against change records; on production servers, treat `1102` as incident-worthy by default.
sim-t1070-001-atomic-windows-event-log-c.production_log_sources: Security `1102` (Security log cleared — generated *by the act of clearing*), System `104` (other logs cleared), Sysmon EID 1 (`wevtutil.exe cl`).
sim-t1070-001-atomic-windows-event-log-c.title: Atomic event: Windows event log cleared

sim-t1070-004-atomic-suspicious-file-del.adversary_activity: Deleting payloads, tools, and artifacts removes forensic evidence (`del`, `sdelete`, `Remove-Item -Force`, secure wipe). The fixture emits a Sysmon `23 FileDelete`.
sim-t1070-004-atomic-suspicious-file-del.detection_logic: Alert on deletion of recently-created executables/scripts from `\Temp\`/`\Downloads\`, on `sdelete`/secure-wipe usage, on a process deleting its own image, and on deletion of forensic artifacts (prefetch, logs). The self-delete-after-execution pattern is high-value.
sim-t1070-004-atomic-suspicious-file-del.discriminators_tuning: File deletion is extremely high-volume and benign by default — this is only useful when tightly scoped (self-delete, wipe tools, deletion of just-dropped payloads). Do not alert on deletion broadly.
sim-t1070-004-atomic-suspicious-file-del.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 23 (FileDelete — can archive the deleted file), EID 26 (FileDeleteDetected), Security `4663`/`4660` (delete access), EDR file telemetry.
sim-t1070-004-atomic-suspicious-file-del.title: Atomic event: Suspicious file deletion

sim-t1071-controlled-beacon.adversary_activity: C2 over standard application protocols (HTTP/HTTPS/DNS) blends malicious traffic into normal web/DNS flows. This plan prepares a fixed benign callback from a lab agent to a controlled domain.
sim-t1071-controlled-beacon.detection_logic: Hunt for beaconing behavior: regular call-home intervals (low jitter), consistent small request/response sizes, connections to rare/young domains, uncommon User-Agents, and high-entropy DNS subdomains. No single packet is malicious — periodicity and rarity are the signal.
sim-t1071-controlled-beacon.discriminators_tuning: Software-update checks, telemetry, and CDNs beacon legitimately — baseline known callback destinations and weight on domain rarity/age plus regularity.
sim-t1071-controlled-beacon.production_log_sources: DNS resolver logs, web proxy logs, NDR/flow metadata, EDR network-connection events, TLS metadata (JA3/JA4, SNI).
sim-t1071-controlled-beacon.title: Controlled HTTP/DNS beacon validation plan

sim-t1071-web-beacon.adversary_activity: A web-based implant beacons over HTTP(S) with periodic GET/POST check-ins to retrieve tasks and return results. The simulation sends a short fixed sequence of beacon-shaped requests and records timing/sequence.
sim-t1071-web-beacon.detection_logic: Alert on regular periodic requests to the same URI/host with consistent sizing and uncommon User-Agent, on a sequence parameter incrementing across check-ins, and on small POST bodies returning small responses at a fixed cadence. Beacon periodicity + destination rarity is the core detection.
sim-t1071-web-beacon.discriminators_tuning: Polling apps, dashboards, and telemetry beacon legitimately — baseline destinations/cadence and weight on rare destinations with default tooling fingerprints.
sim-t1071-web-beacon.production_log_sources: Web proxy logs, NGINX/web access logs (`uri`, `user-agent`, request/response sizes, timing), NDR.
sim-t1071-web-beacon.title: HTTP web beacon canary

sim-t1078-atomic-valid-account-remote-lo.adversary_activity: Using legitimate (often stolen) credentials, an actor logs on like a real user, blending into normal authentication. The fixture emits a Windows `4624 SuccessfulLogon`.
sim-t1078-atomic-valid-account-remote-lo.detection_logic: Valid-account abuse is detected by *anomaly*, not by the event itself: impossible travel, first-seen source IP/host for the account, off-hours logon, dormant-account reactivation, privileged logon from a workstation that account never uses, or a successful `4624` immediately after a `4625` burst (post-spray). Focus on `LogonType` 10 (RemoteInteractive) and 3 (Network).
sim-t1078-atomic-valid-account-remote-lo.discriminators_tuning: This is the hardest technique to alert on cleanly — `4624` is one of the highest-volume events. Use behavioral/UEBA baselining per account; do not alert on raw logons.
sim-t1078-atomic-valid-account-remote-lo.production_log_sources: Windows Security log `4624` (with `LogonType`, `IpAddress`, `WorkstationName`, `LogonProcessName`), IdP/SSO sign-in logs, VPN logs. Pair with `4768`/`4769` (Kerberos) and `4625` (failures) for context.
sim-t1078-atomic-valid-account-remote-lo.title: Atomic event: Valid account remote interactive logon

sim-t1082-system-discovery.adversary_activity: Profiling the host — OS, patch level, hardware, domain — via `systeminfo`, `hostname`, `ver`, `wmic os get`, `wmic qfe`. The fixture records process telemetry for the common discovery sequence.
sim-t1082-system-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on the **discovery sequence** — `whoami` + `systeminfo` + `ipconfig` in rapid succession is a classic post-access recon fingerprint — and on `systeminfo` by service/web accounts.
sim-t1082-system-discovery.discriminators_tuning: Inventory/asset agents run `systeminfo`/`wmic` — baseline those agents and weight on sequenced multi-command recon.
sim-t1082-system-discovery.title: System information discovery

sim-t1083-file-discovery.adversary_activity: Enumerating the filesystem to find data to steal — `dir /s`, `Get-ChildItem -Recurse`, `tree`, `find` — often searching for documents, keys, or credential files. The fixture records telemetry without touching real files.
sim-t1083-file-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on recursive enumeration with content filters (`dir /s *.docx`, `Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Include *.kdbx,*.pem`, `find / -name id_rsa`), and on broad filesystem walks by service accounts. Searching for credential/key extensions is high-value.
sim-t1083-file-discovery.discriminators_tuning: Backup, indexing, and AV scan the filesystem — allowlist those and weight on targeted extension searches and unusual accounts.
sim-t1083-file-discovery.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (recursive `dir`/`Get-ChildItem`), Security `4688`, `auditd execve` (`find`/`ls -R`); high-volume `4663`/Sysmon-11 reads for the actual traversal.
sim-t1083-file-discovery.title: File and directory discovery

sim-t1090-atomic-proxy-tool-network-conn.adversary_activity: Traffic is relayed through a proxy/tunnel to obscure its true source/destination — Tor, `plink` SSH tunnels, `chisel`, `ngrok`, `socat`, SOCKS proxies. The fixture emits a Sysmon `3 NetworkConnection`.
sim-t1090-atomic-proxy-tool-network-conn.detection_logic: Alert on network connections from known tunneling/proxy tool images (`plink.exe`, `chisel`, `ngrok`, `socat`, Tor), on connections to known Tor/ngrok infrastructure, and on local listeners opened on SOCKS-style ports by unexpected processes.
sim-t1090-atomic-proxy-tool-network-conn.discriminators_tuning: Corporate proxies and some dev tools tunnel legitimately — allowlist sanctioned proxy infrastructure and alert on unsanctioned tunneling binaries and destinations.
sim-t1090-atomic-proxy-tool-network-conn.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 3 (NetworkConnection: `Image`, `DestinationIp`/`Port`, `DestinationHostname`), EID 1 (the tool launching), firewall/proxy logs, NDR.
sim-t1090-atomic-proxy-tool-network-conn.title: Atomic event: Proxy tool network connection

sim-t1098-atomic-account-added-to-admin-.adversary_activity: Adding a controlled account to a privileged group (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, local Administrators) grants durable elevated access. The fixture emits a Windows Security `4728 MemberAddedToSecurityEnabledGlobalGroup`.
sim-t1098-atomic-account-added-to-admin-.detection_logic: Alert on additions to high-value groups — `Domain Admins`, `Enterprise Admins`, `Administrators`, `Schema Admins`, `Backup Operators`, `Account Operators` — and especially on additions performed outside change windows, by non-PIM/non-IAM accounts, or immediately after a fresh logon.
sim-t1098-atomic-account-added-to-admin-.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate onboarding adds members to groups — scope alerting to the most sensitive groups and correlate with a ticket/PIM activation. Sensitive-group changes are inherently low-volume and high-value.
sim-t1098-atomic-account-added-to-admin-.production_log_sources: Security `4728` (added to global group), `4732` (added to local group), `4756` (added to universal group), `4733`/`4729` (removals for context); Azure AD/Entra audit logs for cloud role assignments.
sim-t1098-atomic-account-added-to-admin-.title: Atomic event: Account added to privileged group

sim-t1102-atomic-web-service-c2-user-age.adversary_activity: C2 is tunneled through a legitimate web service (GitHub, Pastebin, Google Drive, Telegram, Discord, Slack) so traffic goes to a trusted, allowlisted domain. The fixture emits a structured proxy `HTTP_REQUEST`/`ProxyWebRequest` event.
sim-t1102-atomic-web-service-c2-user-age.detection_logic: Alert on programmatic access patterns to web services — non-browser User-Agents hitting `raw.githubusercontent.com`/`pastebin.com`/`api.telegram.org`/Discord webhooks, regular polling of a specific gist/paste/channel, and unusual upload/download volumes to consumer cloud APIs from server hosts.
sim-t1102-atomic-web-service-c2-user-age.discriminators_tuning: These services are heavily used legitimately — this is genuinely hard; weight on host role (a server beaconing to Pastebin is suspicious), non-browser UAs, and periodicity rather than the destination alone.
sim-t1102-atomic-web-service-c2-user-age.production_log_sources: Web proxy logs (destination domain, URL, User-Agent, bytes), NDR, DNS logs, TLS SNI/JA3.
sim-t1102-atomic-web-service-c2-user-age.title: Atomic event: Web service used as C2 channel

sim-t1105-certutil-transfer.adversary_activity: LOLBins download additional tooling to evade allowlisting — `certutil -urlcache -f http://...`, `bitsadmin /transfer`, `Start-BitsTransfer`, PowerShell `DownloadFile`/`Invoke-WebRequest`. The fixture records command lines without downloading anything.
sim-t1105-certutil-transfer.detection_logic: Alert on `certutil` with `-urlcache`/`-f`/`-split` plus an http(s) URL, on `bitsadmin /transfer` or `Start-BitsTransfer` pulling from external URLs, and on these LOLBins making outbound connections + writing executables. Certutil with a URL is almost never legitimate.
sim-t1105-certutil-transfer.discriminators_tuning: Certutil's normal certificate operations don't use URLs; BITS is used by Windows Update/legitimate updaters — allowlist update services and alert on URL-bearing certutil and ad-hoc BITS transfers to non-update destinations.
sim-t1105-certutil-transfer.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`certutil.exe`/`bitsadmin.exe` with URL), EID 3 (their network connection), EID 11 (downloaded file), BITS-Client/Operational (`59`/`60`), Security `4688`.
sim-t1105-certutil-transfer.title: Certutil and BITS ingress tool transfer

sim-t1105-web-upload-download.adversary_activity: Tooling is staged via plain HTTP upload/download. The simulation requests a fixed benign download path and POSTs a small benign body to an upload endpoint.
sim-t1105-web-upload-download.detection_logic: Alert on downloads of executable/script content from uncommon paths, on uploads of executable content to web endpoints, and on raw-HTTP-client User-Agents transferring binaries. Pair the download with subsequent execution on the endpoint.
sim-t1105-web-upload-download.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate software distribution transfers binaries — weight on tool User-Agents, executable content types, and execution follow-through.
sim-t1105-web-upload-download.production_log_sources: Web access logs (download path, upload endpoint, body length, User-Agent), proxy logs, NDR, EID 11 on the receiving host.
sim-t1105-web-upload-download.title: Ingress tool transfer upload/download canary

sim-t1106-atomic-suspicious-native-api-c.adversary_activity: Malware invokes the Windows Native API (`Nt*`/`Zw*`) or direct syscalls to allocate, write, and execute memory while bypassing higher-level (often hooked) Win32 APIs — e.g., `NtAllocateVirtualMemory` → `NtWriteVirtualMemory` → `NtCreateThreadEx`. The fixture emits a structured EDR `API_CALL` event.
sim-t1106-atomic-suspicious-native-api-c.detection_logic: Alert on the classic injection API triad in one process within a short window, on execution from unbacked/private RWX memory, and on direct syscall stubs that bypass `ntdll` hooks. This detection lives entirely in your EDR's behavioral engine.
sim-t1106-atomic-suspicious-native-api-c.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate runtimes (JIT compilers, .NET, debuggers, AV) allocate executable memory — weight on unbacked memory + remote-thread combinations and process reputation.
sim-t1106-atomic-suspicious-native-api-c.production_log_sources: EDR/kernel telemetry (this is **not** visible in Sysmon or Windows event logs) — API/syscall tracing, memory-region telemetry, ETW Threat Intelligence provider.
sim-t1106-atomic-suspicious-native-api-c.title: Atomic event: Suspicious Native API sequence

sim-t1110-basic-auth-bruteforce.adversary_activity: Brute forcing HTTP Basic auth sends many `Authorization: Basic` headers until one succeeds. The simulation sends two failed and one known-good lab request; the stored `Authorization` header is redacted.
sim-t1110-basic-auth-bruteforce.detection_logic: Alert on a cluster of `401`s carrying `Authorization: Basic` from one source followed by a `200`, on the same source rotating credentials against a Basic-protected path, and on Basic auth over plaintext HTTP. The 401-burst-then-200 shape is the signature.
sim-t1110-basic-auth-bruteforce.discriminators_tuning: Misconfigured clients retry Basic auth — threshold the failure count and require the success transition for higher confidence.
sim-t1110-basic-auth-bruteforce.production_log_sources: Web access logs (presence of `Authorization` header, `401` then `200`), auth logs, WAF logs.
sim-t1110-basic-auth-bruteforce.title: HTTP Basic authentication brute-force sequence

sim-t1110-lab-login-sequence.adversary_activity: Brute forcing tries many passwords against an identity/SSO target. This plan validates telemetry for a low-rate failed-login sequence against a lab test account.
sim-t1110-lab-login-sequence.detection_logic: Alert on a high count of authentication failures for a single account or from a single source within a window, on failures followed by a success, and on MFA-fatigue patterns (many push denials). Track failures per-account and per-source independently.
sim-t1110-lab-login-sequence.discriminators_tuning: Expired passwords and misconfigured clients cause failure bursts — set thresholds above normal app retry noise and weight on the failure-then-success transition.
sim-t1110-lab-login-sequence.production_log_sources: IdP/SSO sign-in logs, MFA logs, VPN logs, Security `4625` (failed logon), `4771` (Kerberos pre-auth failed), SIEM identity alerts.
sim-t1110-lab-login-sequence.title: Lab-only failed-login sequence plan

sim-t1110-web-bruteforce.adversary_activity: Many passwords are tried against one account at a form-based login until success. The simulation sends a fixed sequence of failed passwords plus one final known-good lab password.
sim-t1110-web-bruteforce.detection_logic: Alert on N+ failed authentications for the same username from one source within a window, followed by a success — the classic single-account brute-force curve. Use the auth log's per-attempt outcome.
sim-t1110-web-bruteforce.discriminators_tuning: Lock-out policies blunt real brute force, so attackers go low-and-slow — combine short-window thresholds with longer-window cumulative counts per account.
sim-t1110-web-bruteforce.production_log_sources: Application auth logs (same username, repeated failures, final success), web access logs, WAF brute-force alerts.
sim-t1110-web-bruteforce.title: Web login brute-force sequence

sim-t1110-web-login-failures.adversary_activity: Repeated failed logins against a web login endpoint, the web-app form of brute force. The simulation sends three fixed failed-login canaries without authenticating.
sim-t1110-web-login-failures.detection_logic: Alert on repeated failed POSTs to the login endpoint from one source/for one user, on a tight failure cadence, and on the failure-to-success transition. The catalog's `outcome` and `failure reason` fields drive this.
sim-t1110-web-login-failures.discriminators_tuning: Forgotten passwords produce small bursts — threshold above human-retry levels and correlate by source IP and User-Agent.
sim-t1110-web-login-failures.production_log_sources: Application auth logs (username, outcome, failure reason), web access logs (POST to login, `401`/`403`/`200`), WAF logs.
sim-t1110-web-login-failures.title: Web failed-login sequence canary

sim-t1110-web-password-spray.adversary_activity: Password spraying tries one or a few common passwords across **many** accounts to avoid lockout — the inverse shape of brute force. The simulation POSTs the same wrong password across a fixed list of lab users; the cleartext password is not logged.
sim-t1110-web-password-spray.detection_logic: Alert when one source attempts authentication against many distinct accounts with few attempts per account in a window — high *username cardinality* per source is the spray signature (distinct from brute force's high attempts-per-user). A single success amid the spray is critical.
sim-t1110-web-password-spray.discriminators_tuning: Shared service hosts and misconfigured SSO can fan out across accounts — baseline normal per-source account diversity and weight on the breadth + low-per-user pattern.
sim-t1110-web-password-spray.production_log_sources: Application auth logs (many distinct usernames, ~1 attempt each, shared timing/source), web access logs, IdP sign-in logs for the cloud equivalent.
sim-t1110-web-password-spray.title: Web password spraying sequence

sim-t1112-atomic-registry-security-setti.adversary_activity: Editing security-relevant registry values weakens defenses or enables an attack — disabling UAC (`EnableLUA=0`), disabling Defender keys, weakening LSA protections, enabling WDigest credential caching (`UseLogonCredential=1`). The fixture emits a Sysmon `13 RegistryValueSet`.
sim-t1112-atomic-registry-security-setti.detection_logic: Alert on writes to a curated list of security-sensitive values: `...\Policies\System\EnableLUA`, `...\WDigest\UseLogonCredential=1`, `...\Lsa\RunAsPPL` cleared, Defender `DisableAntiSpyware`/`DisableRealtimeMonitoring`, `...\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender`. Each maps to a specific weakening.
sim-t1112-atomic-registry-security-setti.discriminators_tuning: GPO and legitimate hardening change these — correlate with GPO deployment and alert on the *weakening* direction (e.g., enabling WDigest cleartext) specifically.
sim-t1112-atomic-registry-security-setti.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 13 (RegistryValueSet: `TargetObject`, `Details`), Security `4657` (registry value modified, with auditing).
sim-t1112-atomic-registry-security-setti.title: Atomic event: Security-sensitive registry modification

sim-t1113-atomic-screen-capture-api.adversary_activity: Capturing the screen to collect on-screen data (`BitBlt`, `PrintWindow`, `GetDC`, DirectX capture), often periodically. The fixture emits a structured EDR `SCREEN_CAPTURE` event.
sim-t1113-atomic-screen-capture-api.detection_logic: Alert on screen-capture API use by background/non-GUI processes, on periodic capture loops, and on capture by a process that also has network/staging behavior. Image files written to temp by a non-imaging app reinforce it.
sim-t1113-atomic-screen-capture-api.discriminators_tuning: Conferencing (Teams/Zoom), screenshot tools, and RMM legitimately capture screens — allowlist those and weight on headless/background processes.
sim-t1113-atomic-screen-capture-api.production_log_sources: EDR API/behavioral telemetry (screen-capture API calls) — **not in Sysmon/Windows logs**; EID 11 for written screenshot files.
sim-t1113-atomic-screen-capture-api.title: Atomic event: Screen capture API use

sim-t1115-atomic-clipboard-read.adversary_activity: Reading or monitoring the clipboard captures copied passwords, crypto addresses, and sensitive text (`GetClipboardData`, clipboard-change monitoring, `Get-Clipboard`). Clipper malware also swaps copied crypto addresses. The fixture emits a structured EDR `CLIPBOARD_READ` event.
sim-t1115-atomic-clipboard-read.detection_logic: Alert on clipboard reads/monitoring by background processes, on tight clipboard-polling loops, and on processes that read the clipboard then modify it (clipper behavior). Combine with network behavior for exfil context.
sim-t1115-atomic-clipboard-read.discriminators_tuning: Clipboard managers, password managers, RDP/VDI, and office apps access the clipboard — allowlist and weight on background/no-UI processes and polling loops.
sim-t1115-atomic-clipboard-read.production_log_sources: EDR API/behavioral telemetry (clipboard access/monitoring), Sysmon EID 1 (`Get-Clipboard`/`pbpaste`).
sim-t1115-atomic-clipboard-read.title: Atomic event: Clipboard data access

sim-t1123-atomic-audio-capture-api.adversary_activity: Recording the microphone for surveillance (`waveIn*`, WASAPI capture, `winmm`). The fixture emits a structured EDR `AUDIO_CAPTURE`/`MicrophoneAccess` event.
sim-t1123-atomic-audio-capture-api.detection_logic: Alert on microphone access by a process that is not a known conferencing/recording app, and on capture by a background/headless process. Audio capture paired with file-write + network is the spyware chain.
sim-t1123-atomic-audio-capture-api.discriminators_tuning: Teams/Zoom/voice tools and OS assistants use the mic — allowlist them and weight on unexpected apps.
sim-t1123-atomic-audio-capture-api.production_log_sources: EDR API/behavioral telemetry (microphone capture), OS privacy/capability logs where available.
sim-t1123-atomic-audio-capture-api.title: Atomic event: Audio capture API use

sim-t1125-atomic-video-capture-api.adversary_activity: Accessing the webcam to record video/images for surveillance. The fixture emits a structured EDR `VIDEO_CAPTURE`/`CameraAccess` event.
sim-t1125-atomic-video-capture-api.detection_logic: Alert on camera access by non-conferencing/non-camera apps, by background processes, or right after a suspicious download. Webcam access by an unknown binary is high-value spyware signal.
sim-t1125-atomic-video-capture-api.discriminators_tuning: Video-conferencing and camera utilities use the webcam — allowlist them; the noise is otherwise low.
sim-t1125-atomic-video-capture-api.production_log_sources: EDR API/behavioral telemetry (camera access), OS camera-privacy logs where available.
sim-t1125-atomic-video-capture-api.title: Atomic event: Video capture API use

sim-t1133-remote-service-reachability.adversary_activity: Adversaries gain initial access through exposed remote-access services — VPN, RDP, SSH, Citrix — using valid or stolen credentials. This plan validates telemetry for a single, credential-less reachability check against an approved service/port.
sim-t1133-remote-service-reachability.detection_logic: Alert on successful external authentications to remote-access services from new geolocations/ASNs, on impossible-travel, on logins to dormant accounts, and on external sources reaching internal-only services. Connection attempts without credentials map to reachability probing.
sim-t1133-remote-service-reachability.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate remote workers and third-party vendors connect externally — baseline normal source geos/ASNs per account and alert on deviation, not on remote access itself.
sim-t1133-remote-service-reachability.production_log_sources: VPN/gateway logs, firewall allow/deny logs, RDP (`4624` LogonType 10), SSH (`/var/log/auth.log`, `sshd`), Citrix/VDI logs, IdP sign-in logs.
sim-t1133-remote-service-reachability.title:
Web shell URI and POST canary validation

(see also T1505.003)

---

## [T1133](http://localhost:3000/navigator?technique=T1133) - External remote service reachability plan

sim-t1134-atomic-token-impersonation.adversary_activity: An actor duplicates or impersonates another account's access token (`DuplicateTokenEx` + `ImpersonateLoggedOnUser`, `runas /netonly`, "potato" SeImpersonate abuse) to act in a different security context — frequently to reach SYSTEM. The fixture emits a structured EDR `TOKEN_IMPERSONATION` event.
sim-t1134-atomic-token-impersonation.detection_logic: Alert on `4624` LogonType 9 from unusual processes, on token-duplication API sequences targeting SYSTEM/admin tokens, and on a service account with `SeImpersonatePrivilege` spawning a SYSTEM process (potato pattern). Correlate token impersonation with a subsequent high-integrity action.
sim-t1134-atomic-token-impersonation.discriminators_tuning: IIS/SQL service accounts legitimately hold `SeImpersonate` — weight on the resulting SYSTEM shell or anomalous child rather than the privilege itself.
sim-t1134-atomic-token-impersonation.production_log_sources: EDR token-operation telemetry, Security `4624` LogonType **9** (NewCredentials, the `runas /netonly` signature), `4672` (special privileges assigned), `4673`/`4674` (privileged service/operation).
sim-t1134-atomic-token-impersonation.title: Atomic event: Access token impersonation

sim-t1135-atomic-network-share-discovery.adversary_activity: Enumerating SMB shares to find data and lateral footholds — `net view \\host`, `net share`, `Get-SmbShare`, `Get-SmbConnection`. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1135-atomic-network-share-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on `net view \\<host>` against multiple hosts, on `net share`/`Get-SmbShare` by service accounts, and on share enumeration in a discovery burst preceding share access.
sim-t1135-atomic-network-share-discovery.discriminators_tuning: Admin and backup tooling enumerate shares — context and breadth are the discriminators.
sim-t1135-atomic-network-share-discovery.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`net.exe view`/`share`, PowerShell `Get-Smb*`), Security `4688`; on file servers, `5140`/`5145` for the resulting access.
sim-t1135-atomic-network-share-discovery.title: Atomic event: Network share discovery

sim-t1140-atomic-certutil-decode.adversary_activity: `certutil -decode`/`-decodehex` is abused to turn a base64/hex-encoded dropped file back into an executable payload (a LOLBin deobfuscation step). The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1140-atomic-certutil-decode.detection_logic: Alert on `certutil.exe` with `-decode`/`-decodehex`/`-urlcache` arguments, particularly writing output to `\Temp\`/`\Users\` or producing an executable. Pair the decode with a subsequent execution of the output file.
sim-t1140-atomic-certutil-decode.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate certutil use centers on certificate operations and rarely uses `-decode` against arbitrary files — the decode/encode verbs are the discriminator.
sim-t1140-atomic-certutil-decode.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`Image=certutil.exe`, `CommandLine`), Security `4688`, EID 11 (the decoded output file created).
sim-t1140-atomic-certutil-decode.title: Atomic event: File deobfuscation with certutil decode

sim-t1190-sqli-xss-canary.adversary_activity: SQL injection manipulates query parameters to alter backend SQL (`' OR 1=1--`, `UNION SELECT`, `; DROP`); cross-site scripting injects markup/script (`<script>`, `onerror=`, `javascript:`) to run in victims' browsers. The simulation sends one SQLi-shaped and one XSS-shaped request to benign endpoints with no DB or browser execution.
sim-t1190-sqli-xss-canary.detection_logic: Alert on SQL meta-syntax in parameters (`UNION SELECT`, `OR 1=1`, `--`, `/*`, `xp_cmdshell`, `SLEEP(`), and on HTML/JS injection markers (`<script`, `onerror=`, `onload=`, `%3Cscript`). Database errors or `500`s following a SQLi-shaped request indicate impact. URL-decode before matching.
sim-t1190-sqli-xss-canary.discriminators_tuning: Search boxes, code-snippet fields, and security forums legitimately contain these strings — bind the rule to parameters that reach a database/render context, and weight on response anomalies (DB errors, reflected payload).
sim-t1190-sqli-xss-canary.production_log_sources: Web access logs and query strings, WAF logs, app/DB error logs, structured web JSON.
sim-t1190-sqli-xss-canary.title: SQL injection and XSS canary validation

sim-t1190-ssrf-canary.adversary_activity: Server-Side Request Forgery tricks the server into making outbound requests the attacker controls — classically to the cloud metadata service (`169.254.169.254`) to steal instance credentials, or to internal/loopback addresses to reach services behind the perimeter. The simulation records SSRF-shaped URL parameters but the server never fetches them.
sim-t1190-ssrf-canary.detection_logic: Alert on URL-valued parameters pointing at `169.254.169.254`, `metadata.google.internal`, `localhost`/`127.0.0.1`, `0.0.0.0`, `[::1]`, RFC1918 ranges, or `file://`/`gopher://` schemes. The strongest signal in production is the **server making an unexpected outbound request to the metadata IP** — monitor egress from app servers.
sim-t1190-ssrf-canary.discriminators_tuning: Webhook, image-fetch, and link-preview features legitimately fetch user-supplied URLs — allowlist their destinations and alert on internal/metadata targets specifically.
sim-t1190-ssrf-canary.production_log_sources: Web access logs (URL parameters), WAF logs, app logs, and crucially **egress/proxy/firewall logs from the server itself** (to see the forged outbound request).
sim-t1190-ssrf-canary.title: SSRF metadata and loopback canary validation

sim-t1190-traversal-canary.adversary_activity: Directory/path traversal abuses unsanitized path parameters (`../../etc/passwd`, `..%2f`, `....//`) to read files outside the web root — credentials, configs, source. The simulation sends traversal-shaped strings with explicit canary markers and never resolves them.
sim-t1190-traversal-canary.detection_logic: Alert on encoded/decoded traversal sequences in URI or query — `../`, `..\`, `%2e%2e%2f`, `%252e`, `....//` — and on requests targeting sensitive filenames (`/etc/passwd`, `web.config`, `.env`). A `200` to a traversal-shaped request is high-severity (likely successful read).
sim-t1190-traversal-canary.discriminators_tuning: Some apps legitimately use `..` in non-filesystem contexts; decode before matching to catch double-encoding, and prioritize requests that combine traversal syntax with known sensitive filenames.
sim-t1190-traversal-canary.production_log_sources: Web access logs (`uri`, query string, `status`), WAF logs, app logs.
sim-t1190-traversal-canary.title: Path traversal canary validation

sim-t1190-web-exposure.adversary_activity: Initial access against internet-facing apps starts with confirming exposure and reachable paths. This plan entry reviews target tech and allowed paths and starts the local telemetry server — no exploit payload is sent.
sim-t1190-web-exposure.detection_logic: No standalone alert. Use as baseline visibility: ensure every externally reachable path is logged with method, status, and source. Hunt for first-seen external sources hitting sensitive endpoints.
sim-t1190-web-exposure.discriminators_tuning: Pure visibility validation; benign by design.
sim-t1190-web-exposure.production_log_sources: Web access logs, WAF logs, app logs, perimeter firewall/netflow.
sim-t1190-web-exposure.title: Public web exposure validation plan

sim-t1203-atomic-office-spawned-script-h.adversary_activity: A malicious document or web content exploits the client application (Office, browser, PDF reader) so the application process spawns a script host or shell — e.g., `winword.exe` → `cmd.exe`/`powershell.exe`/`wscript.exe`. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1203-atomic-office-spawned-script-h.detection_logic: This is one of the cleanest endpoint detections: alert when an Office app, PDF reader, or browser (`winword.exe`, `excel.exe`, `powerpnt.exe`, `outlook.exe`, `acrord32.exe`, browser renderers) is the **parent** of `cmd.exe`, `powershell.exe`, `wscript.exe`, `cscript.exe`, `mshta.exe`, `rundll32.exe`, or `regsvr32.exe`. Microsoft's ASR "block Office child process" rule covers the same logic.
sim-t1203-atomic-office-spawned-script-h.discriminators_tuning: A handful of legitimate add-ins and document-automation workflows spawn children — baseline and allowlist specific signed add-in chains; the noise is low.
sim-t1203-atomic-office-spawned-script-h.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`ParentImage`, `Image`, `CommandLine`), EDR process-lineage telemetry, Defender ASR events.
sim-t1203-atomic-office-spawned-script-h.title: Atomic event: Exploitation for client execution artifact

sim-t1204-002-atomic-user-executed-downl.adversary_activity: A user is socially engineered into running a downloaded executable/script. The file carries Mark-of-the-Web and its parent process is a browser, email client, or Explorer. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1204-002-atomic-user-executed-downl.detection_logic: Alert when a process executes from a download/Temp path with a `ParentImage` of a browser (`chrome.exe`, `msedge.exe`, `firefox.exe`), email client (`outlook.exe`), or archive tool, **and** the file carries a MOTW zone of 3 (Internet). High-risk patterns: `.exe`/`.scr`/`.js`/`.hta` run from `\Downloads\` or `\AppData\Local\Temp\`.
sim-t1204-002-atomic-user-executed-downl.discriminators_tuning: Users legitimately run installers from Downloads — weight on file type, signing status, MOTW, and whether the binary is known-good. Newly-seen unsigned binaries from email-client parents are the highest-fidelity slice.
sim-t1204-002-atomic-user-executed-downl.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (ProcessCreate: `Image`, `ParentImage`, `User`), Sysmon EID 11 (FileCreate in Downloads), Sysmon EID 15 (FileCreateStreamHash — MOTW `Zone.Identifier`), EDR process tree, browser download logs.
sim-t1204-002-atomic-user-executed-downl.title: Atomic event: User execution of downloaded file

sim-t1218-regsvr32-proxy.adversary_activity: `regsvr32.exe` (a trusted Microsoft binary) is abused to execute a remote scriptlet via the "Squiblydoo" technique (`regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:http://.../file.sct scrobj.dll`), bypassing application allowlisting. The fixture records command lines without registering DLLs or fetching scriptlets.
sim-t1218-regsvr32-proxy.detection_logic: Alert on `regsvr32.exe` with `/i:` referencing an **http(s)/URL** or a `.sct` scriptlet, with `scrobj.dll`, or making an outbound network connection (regsvr32 normally never touches the network). The `/s /n /u /i:` combination is the Squiblydoo signature.
sim-t1218-regsvr32-proxy.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate COM registration uses local DLL paths with no URL and no network — URL/scriptlet/network usage cleanly isolates the abuse.
sim-t1218-regsvr32-proxy.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`Image=regsvr32.exe`, `CommandLine`), EID 3 (NetworkConnection from regsvr32), Security `4688`.
sim-t1218-regsvr32-proxy.title: Regsvr32 signed binary proxy execution

sim-t1218-rundll32-proxy.adversary_activity: `rundll32.exe` proxies execution of arbitrary DLLs/exports or inline script (`rundll32 javascript:...`, `rundll32 <dll>,<export>`, suspicious ordinals) to evade allowlisting. The fixture records command lines without loading anything.
sim-t1218-rundll32-proxy.detection_logic: Alert on `rundll32.exe` with `javascript:`/`vbscript:`/`mshtml`, with a DLL in a user-writable path, with no/odd arguments, calling exports by ordinal, or making network connections. `rundll32` spawned by Office or with a URL is high-value.
sim-t1218-rundll32-proxy.discriminators_tuning: `rundll32` is used heavily by legitimate Windows components — baseline normal DLL/export pairs; the inline-script and user-path-DLL variants are the cleanest signals.
sim-t1218-rundll32-proxy.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`Image=rundll32.exe`, `CommandLine`, `ParentImage`), EID 7 (DLL loaded), EID 3 (network), Security `4688`.
sim-t1218-rundll32-proxy.title: Rundll32 signed binary proxy execution

sim-t1219-atomic-remote-access-software-.adversary_activity: Attackers (and ransomware affiliates) install legitimate remote-access/RMM software for hands-on-keyboard control — AnyDesk, TeamViewer, ScreenConnect/ConnectWise, Atera, Splashtop, RemoteUtilities. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1219-atomic-remote-access-software-.detection_logic: Alert on execution of RMM binaries that are **not your sanctioned tool**, on RMM software running from temp/user paths, and on connections to RMM vendor domains from hosts that shouldn't have that tool. Unsanctioned-RMM is a leading ransomware-precursor signal.
sim-t1219-atomic-remote-access-software-.discriminators_tuning: Your approved RMM is legitimate everywhere — maintain a strict allowlist of the one(s) you sanction and alert on every other RMM family. --- # Exfiltration
sim-t1219-atomic-remote-access-software-.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (RMM binary names/paths), EID 3 (connections to RMM cloud domains), Security `4688`, proxy/DNS logs for RMM infrastructure.
sim-t1219-atomic-remote-access-software-.title: Atomic event: Remote access software execution

sim-t1222-001-atomic-icacls-permission-c.adversary_activity: Modifying file/folder ACLs grants the attacker access or hides changes (`icacls /grant Everyone:F`, `takeown /f`, `cacls`), often to enable persistence or tamper with protected files. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1222-001-atomic-icacls-permission-c.detection_logic: Alert on `icacls`/`cacls` granting `Everyone`/`Users` full control (`:F`) or `takeown` over system/protected paths, and on permission grants targeting startup, service, or security-relevant locations.
sim-t1222-001-atomic-icacls-permission-c.discriminators_tuning: Installers and admin scripts adjust ACLs — weight on overly-permissive grants (`Everyone:F`) and on protected-path targets.
sim-t1222-001-atomic-icacls-permission-c.title: Atomic event: Windows file permission modification

sim-t1482-atomic-domain-trust-discovery.adversary_activity: Mapping Active Directory trust relationships to plan cross-domain movement — `nltest /domain_trusts`, `Get-ADTrust`, `dsquery * -filter trust`, BloodHound collection. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1482-atomic-domain-trust-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on `nltest /domain_trusts`/`/all_trusts`, `Get-ADTrust`, and LDAP queries for `trustedDomain` objects — especially from non-admin hosts/accounts. Trust enumeration is a strong pre-lateral-movement signal.
sim-t1482-atomic-domain-trust-discovery.discriminators_tuning: AD admins run these occasionally — scope to non-admin contexts and correlate with broader recon (BloodHound-style LDAP bursts).
sim-t1482-atomic-domain-trust-discovery.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`nltest.exe /domain_trusts`, PowerShell AD cmdlets), Security `4688`; on DCs, `4662` (directory access to trust objects).
sim-t1482-atomic-domain-trust-discovery.title: Atomic event: Domain trust discovery

sim-t1486-atomic-ransomware-file-rename.adversary_activity: Ransomware encrypts files at scale and renames them (new extension), then drops ransom notes — a high-velocity mass-modification of files. The fixture emits a structured EDR `RANSOMWARE_CANARY`/`MassFileRename` event.
sim-t1486-atomic-ransomware-file-rename.detection_logic: Alert on a single process modifying/renaming a large number of files across directories in a very short window, on a flood of files gaining the same new extension, on ransom-note filenames appearing in many folders, and on access to honeypot/canary files. Often co-occurs with shadow-copy deletion (T1490) — correlate them.
sim-t1486-atomic-ransomware-file-rename.discriminators_tuning: Bulk legitimate operations (mass rename, backup, encryption tools) exist but rarely at ransomware velocity across user data — rate + breadth + new-extension uniformity make this high-fidelity. Canary files give near-zero-FP early warning.
sim-t1486-atomic-ransomware-file-rename.production_log_sources: EDR mass-file-operation/behavioral telemetry, Sysmon EID 11/23 (rapid file create/delete), Security `4663` bursts, file-server audit logs, canary/honeypot file triggers.
sim-t1486-atomic-ransomware-file-rename.title: Atomic event: Ransomware-like file encryption artifact

sim-t1490-atomic-shadow-copy-deletion.adversary_activity: To prevent recovery before/after encryption, ransomware deletes Volume Shadow Copies and disables recovery — `vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet`, `wmic shadowcopy delete`, `wbadmin delete catalog`, `bcdedit /set recoveryenabled no`, `Delete-Backup`. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1490-atomic-shadow-copy-deletion.detection_logic: Alert on `vssadmin delete shadows`, `wmic shadowcopy delete`, `wbadmin delete catalog/systemstatebackup`, and `bcdedit /set {default} recoveryenabled no` / `bootstatuspolicy ignoreallfailures`. These are extremely high-fidelity ransomware indicators and rarely benign.
sim-t1490-atomic-shadow-copy-deletion.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate backup management occasionally deletes shadow copies — but `bcdedit` recovery-disable and `vssadmin delete /all /quiet` are near-certain malicious; treat as critical and correlate with mass-file activity (T1486). --- # Appendix: coverage and detection-engineering notes **Telemetry-source dependencies.** These detections only work if the underlying source is collected in production. Confirm you have: Sysmon (with a tuned config covering EIDs 1, 3, 7, 8, 10, 11, 13, 19–21, 23), PowerShell Script Block + Module logging, Windows Security auditing (`4624`/`4625`/`4663`/`4688`/`4697`/`4698`/`4728`/`5140`/`5145`/`1102`), Defender Operational, web/proxy logs, DNS logs, firewall/NetFlow, email gateway, and Linux `auditd`. `4663`/`4660` require SACLs to fire; `4688` command-line auditing must be explicitly enabled. **The Sysmon EID 1 caveat (repeated because it matters).** ~20 techniques here resolve to ProcessCreate. For all of them, your detection differentiates entirely on `CommandLine`, `Image`, `OriginalFileName`, and `ParentImage`. Verify clean parsing and consider command-line obfuscation (caret/quote/env-var tricks) when writing matches. **What these simulations validate — and don't.** A pass confirms your SIEM **parses the event schema and your rule logic fires**. It does **not** confirm that a real OS would emit that event, that the detection survives obfuscation/evasion variants, that it won't generate excessive false positives at production volume, or that prevention/blocking controls work — nothing executes on a real system. Treat green results as "rule matches a correctly-shaped event," then validate the remaining three dimensions separately. **Highest-fidelity, lowest-tuning detections** (good first deployments): event-log cleared (`1102`), WMI consumer creation (Sysmon `20`), accessibility-IFEO debugger, password-filter DLL registration, shadow-copy deletion / `bcdedit` recovery-disable, LSASS access by non-allowlisted process, Office-spawns-script-host, and unsanctioned RMM execution. **Coverage gaps to be aware of** (techniques this catalog does not exercise): Impact beyond ransomware (data destruction T1485, disk wipe T1561, service stop T1489, resource hijacking T1496); genuine cloud control-plane and IdP telemetry (the `cloud`/`identity-lab` tags sit on Windows-endpoint fixtures, so CloudTrail/Entra/GCP-audit detections aren't tested); and Resource Development (largely adversary-side, little victim telemetry).
sim-t1490-atomic-shadow-copy-deletion.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`vssadmin.exe`/`wmic.exe`/`wbadmin.exe`/`bcdedit.exe` with delete/disable args), Security `4688`, System events for VSS.
sim-t1490-atomic-shadow-copy-deletion.title: Atomic event: Inhibit system recovery command

sim-t1497-atomic-sandbox-evasion-check.adversary_activity: Malware checks whether it is running in a VM/sandbox before detonating — querying CPU/MAC/firmware artifacts, VM-specific processes/registry, low core/RAM counts, or using timing/sleep delays. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1497-atomic-sandbox-evasion-check.detection_logic: Alert on queries for VM artifacts — `wmic computersystem get model`, `wmic bios`, registry reads for `VMware`/`VBox`/`QEMU`, checks of MAC OUI prefixes, enumeration of VM processes (`vmtoolsd`, `vboxservice`) — especially clustered together shortly after a file's first execution.
sim-t1497-atomic-sandbox-evasion-check.discriminators_tuning: Inventory/asset tools query hardware legitimately — the *clustering* of multiple VM-artifact checks right after execution distinguishes evasion from inventory.
sim-t1497-atomic-sandbox-evasion-check.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (discovery command lines), EDR API telemetry (timing/anti-analysis), Security `4688`.
sim-t1497-atomic-sandbox-evasion-check.title: Atomic event: Virtualization and sandbox evasion check

sim-t1505-suspicious-extension-upload.adversary_activity: The delivery half of a web shell: uploading a server-executable file (`.php`/`.aspx`/`.jsp`) to an upload endpoint so it can later be invoked. The simulation POSTs benign bodies to such paths; no file is persisted.
sim-t1505-suspicious-extension-upload.detection_logic: Alert on POSTs that result in a server-executable extension being written to a web-served directory, on uploads whose declared content-type mismatches a script extension, and on FIM events creating `.php`/`.aspx`/`.jsp`/`.jspx` files in the webroot. A new script file in the webroot followed by a GET to it is the kill chain.
sim-t1505-suspicious-extension-upload.discriminators_tuning: CMS plugin/theme uploads and dev deployments write scripts legitimately — scope to user-upload directories and unexpected webroot writes outside deployment windows.
sim-t1505-suspicious-extension-upload.production_log_sources: Web access logs (POST to upload paths, `status`), structured web JSON (body length/hash), file-integrity monitoring on web directories, Sysmon EID 11 (FileCreate in webroot).
sim-t1505-suspicious-extension-upload.title: Suspicious web extension upload

sim-t1505-webshell-canary.adversary_activity: A web shell is a server-side script (`.php`/`.aspx`/`.jsp`) planted in a web-accessible directory that executes attacker commands passed as parameters (`?cmd=`, `?action=`), giving persistent remote control of the server. The simulation sends web-shell-shaped URIs/POSTs; the server returns benign responses.
sim-t1505-webshell-canary.detection_logic: Alert on requests to script files with command-like parameters (`cmd`, `exec`, `c`, `pass`), on POSTs to recently-created script files, on a script file requested by a single source with no referrer, and — the gold-standard signal — **the web server worker process becoming the parent of a shell**.
sim-t1505-webshell-canary.discriminators_tuning: Legitimate apps execute server-side scripts constantly — the web-server-spawns-shell parent/child relationship and newly-created scripts in the webroot are the discriminators, not script access itself.
sim-t1505-webshell-canary.production_log_sources: Web access logs (`uri`, `method`, `status`, `user-agent`), structured web JSON (body hash/length), and — most decisively — endpoint Sysmon EID 1 showing the web server process (`w3wp.exe`, `httpd`, `php-fpm`, `nginx`, `java`) spawning `cmd.exe`/`/bin/sh`.
sim-t1505-webshell-canary.title: Web shell URI and POST canary validation

sim-t1518-001-atomic-security-software-d.adversary_activity: Specifically identifying AV/EDR/security tooling before acting — `tasklist | findstr` for AV process names, `Get-Service` filtered for security products, `wmic` queries for AntiVirusProduct. The fixture emits a Sysmon `1 ProcessCreate`.
sim-t1518-001-atomic-security-software-d.detection_logic: Alert on process/service enumeration filtered for security-vendor names (`CrowdStrike`, `SentinelOne`, `Defender`/`MsMpEng`, `Carbon`, `cylance`, `sophos`, `wazuh`), and on `wmic /namespace:\\root\SecurityCenter2`. Filtering specifically *for* security tools is a much stronger signal than generic process discovery.
sim-t1518-001-atomic-security-software-d.discriminators_tuning: Rarely benign to grep running processes for EDR names — this is one of the cleaner discovery detections.
sim-t1518-001-atomic-security-software-d.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`CommandLine` containing AV/EDR product/process names), Security `4688`.
sim-t1518-001-atomic-security-software-d.title: Atomic event: Security software discovery

sim-t1518-software-discovery.adversary_activity: Inventorying installed software to find exploitable apps or valuable targets — `wmic product get`, `Get-ItemProperty ...\Uninstall\*`, `reg query` of uninstall keys. The fixture records process telemetry without querying installed software.
sim-t1518-software-discovery.detection_logic: Alert on enumeration of installed-software registry paths and `wmic product get` by service/web accounts or in a recon burst.
sim-t1518-software-discovery.discriminators_tuning: Patch/inventory agents do exactly this — allowlist them and weight on unusual account context.
sim-t1518-software-discovery.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 1 (`wmic product`, PowerShell uninstall-key reads, `reg query`), Security `4688`.
sim-t1518-software-discovery.title: Software discovery

sim-t1539-atomic-browser-cookie-access.adversary_activity: Stealing browser cookies (the `Cookies` SQLite DB) lets an attacker hijack authenticated sessions and bypass MFA via session tokens. The fixture emits a Sysmon `11 FileCreate`.
sim-t1539-atomic-browser-cookie-access.detection_logic: Alert when a non-browser process reads/copies the `Cookies` database for Chrome/Edge/Firefox, especially copying it to a staging/temp path. Cookie theft + outbound transfer chains into session hijacking.
sim-t1539-atomic-browser-cookie-access.discriminators_tuning: Same as the credential store — allowlist browsers and sync agents; alert on foreign processes touching the cookie DB.
sim-t1539-atomic-browser-cookie-access.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 11 (access/copy of `...\Network\Cookies` or `Cookies`), EID 1 (the accessing process), EDR file telemetry.
sim-t1539-atomic-browser-cookie-access.title: Atomic event: Browser cookie database access

sim-t1543-service-creation.adversary_activity: A new Windows service provides SYSTEM-level persistence and auto-start (`sc create`, `New-Service`, direct registry write under `Services\`). The fixture records service-creation command lines without creating a service.
sim-t1543-service-creation.detection_logic: Alert on `sc.exe create`/`New-Service` with a `binPath` pointing to a script host, `cmd /c`, PowerShell, or a binary in `\Temp\`/`\Users\`. Services with auto-start + suspicious binpath + non-standard name are high-value.
sim-t1543-service-creation.discriminators_tuning: Software installs legitimately create services — discriminate on binpath location and content (LOLBins/temp paths) rather than on creation.
sim-t1543-service-creation.production_log_sources: System `7045` (ServiceInstalled), Security `4697`, Sysmon EID 1 (`sc.exe create`) and EID 13 (registry write to `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\`).
sim-t1543-service-creation.title: Windows service creation

sim-t1546-003-atomic-wmi-event-subscript.adversary_activity: A permanent WMI event subscription (an `__EventFilter` + `__EventConsumer` + `FilterToConsumerBinding`) triggers a payload on a system event (boot, time, logon) — a stealthy, fileless, SYSTEM-level persistence. The fixture emits a Sysmon `19 WmiEventFilter`.
sim-t1546-003-atomic-wmi-event-subscript.detection_logic: Alert on any creation of a `CommandLineEventConsumer` or `ActiveScriptEventConsumer` (Sysmon EID 20) — these are rare and high-fidelity. Pair filter+consumer+binding to confirm a persistence subscription; inspect the consumer's command/script for payload content.
sim-t1546-003-atomic-wmi-event-subscript.discriminators_tuning: Very few legitimate products use permanent WMI subscriptions (some SCCM/monitoring) — baseline those and treat everything else as suspicious. One of the highest-fidelity persistence detections available.
sim-t1546-003-atomic-wmi-event-subscript.production_log_sources: Sysmon EID 19 (WmiEventFilter), EID 20 (WmiEventConsumer), EID 21 (WmiEventConsumerToFilter binding) — the three together are the full signal; **WMI-Activity/Operational** log.
sim-t1546-003-atomic-wmi-event-subscript.title: Atomic event: WMI event subscription persistence

sim-t1546-008-atomic-accessibility-featu.adversary_activity: The "sticky keys" class of backdoor either replaces an accessibility binary (`sethc.exe`, `utilman.exe`, `osk.exe`, `Magnify.exe`) with `cmd.exe`, or sets an Image File Execution Options debugger so the accessibility key combo launches a SYSTEM shell from the logon screen. The fixture emits a Sysmon `13 RegistryValueSet`.
sim-t1546-008-atomic-accessibility-featu.detection_logic: Alert on any `Debugger` value set under `...\Image File Execution Options\sethc.exe|utilman.exe|osk.exe|magnify.exe|narrator.exe|displayswitch.exe`, and on modification of those binaries in `System32`. Both are extremely rare legitimately.
